review_summary: This unit supports concept questions about attention scoring variants, including dot-product, bilinear, low-rank, additive, and projected attention.

qa_outputs.qa_rationale: The item is aligned, grounded, and does not need repair after the prior cleanup pass.
The item is aligned, grounded, and does not need repair after the prior cleanup pass.

repaired_question_bank.question: Why can plain dot-product attention be too crude when comparing encoder and decoder hidden states?
choices: It compares the full hidden states directly even though only some stored information may be relevant for lookup.
It cannot be normalized with a softmax.
It only works when the encoder has exactly one hidden state.
It requires every source word to appear in the target sentence.
repaired_question_bank.explanation: Hidden states can contain many kinds of information. A raw dot product treats all dimensions as equally involved in matching, whereas richer scoring can learn which features matter.

repaired_question_bank.question: What does bilinear or multiplicative attention add compared with plain dot-product attention?
choices: A learned matrix that can align relevant features across encoder and decoder representations.
A rule that attention weights must be uniform across all source positions.
A requirement that encoder and decoder states be discrete labels.
A replacement of the softmax with a hard threshold.
repaired_question_bank.explanation: The learned matrix gives the model flexibility to compare particular subspaces or dimensions rather than simply taking an unstructured dot product over the original states.

repaired_question_bank.question: How does a low-rank bilinear attention form connect to transformer-style attention?
choices: It can be viewed as projecting vectors into a lower-dimensional space and then applying dot-product attention.
It removes the need for any keys, queries, or vector projections.
It replaces attention with a recurrent hidden state update.
It forces every attention score to be computed by a separate human-designed rule.
repaired_question_bank.explanation: Factoring the bilinear matrix is equivalent to transforming the vectors first, then comparing them with a dot product. This projected-dot-product idea is central to transformer attention.

item_calibration_bootstrap.calibration_rationale: "Why can plain dot-product attention be too crude when comparing encoder and decoder hidden states" asks for applied understanding rather than pure recall, so a medium prior fits.
